title: Cybersecurity Basics for Social Workers
description: Learn essential cybersecurity practices to protect client data and maintain professional ethics in social work

nodes.title: Cybersecurity in Social Work: Protecting Your Clients
nodes.content: As a social worker, you handle some of the most sensitive information: client histories, contact details, case notes, and personal circumstances. A security breach doesn't just risk your agency's reputation—it directly harms vulnerable clients who trust you with their stories. This course teaches three critical areas of cybersecurity that directly apply to your daily work: protecting client data, securing your access to systems, and recognizing attempts to manipulate you into giving away confidential information. By the end, you'll have practical strategies to prevent breaches and maintain the ethical standards your profession demands.

nodes.title: Choose Your Learning Path
nodes.content: Select which cybersecurity topics are most relevant to your role. You can explore one, two, or all three areas depending on your needs and experience level.

choices.label: Client Data Protection: Securing sensitive records
Access & Password Security: Protecting your accounts

nodes.title: Client Data Protection: Securing Sensitive Records
nodes.content: Client records contain trauma histories, family details, and contact information that could endanger clients if leaked. Data protection means: (1) never leaving devices unattended or unlocked, (2) using encrypted storage for sensitive files, (3) following your agency's data retention and deletion policies, (4) understanding what information truly needs to be stored, and (5) reporting suspected breaches immediately. Every client relationship is built on trust—protecting their data is a non-negotiable ethical duty that directly impacts your community.

nodes.title: Example: The Unlocked Laptop
nodes.content: Maria, a case manager at a mental health nonprofit, stepped away from her desk to meet with a client. Her laptop, containing open case files with client names, addresses, and treatment plans, remained unlocked. A visiting vendor saw the files while walking past. This breach violated client confidentiality and agency policy. Maria learned to: always lock her screen using Windows Key + L, use automatic lock timeouts, and store sensitive files in encrypted folders. Simple habits prevent real harm to vulnerable people.

nodes.title: Access & Password Security: Protecting Your Accounts
nodes.content: Your work account is the gateway to client data. A compromised password means someone else can access your cases, send emails from your account, and potentially modify records. Strong access security requires: (1) unique, complex passwords for work systems never reused from personal accounts, (2) enabling multi-factor authentication (MFA) whenever available, (3) logging out after each session on shared or public devices, (4) never writing passwords down or sharing them with colleagues, and (5) updating passwords if you suspect compromise. Think of your password like your professional credential—only you should ever use it.

nodes.title: Example: The Shared Password Problem
nodes.content: David, a social worker at a youth services agency, reused his simple password ('welcome123') across personal email, social media, and work systems. When his personal email was breached in a data leak, attackers used the same password to access his work account. They changed his password and accessed weeks of case files with vulnerable youth information. David learned: use a password manager to generate unique, complex passwords for each system; enable MFA so password alone isn't enough; and use phrases with numbers and symbols like 'SafeHome#2024Kids' rather than simple words.

nodes.title: Phishing & Social Engineering: Recognizing Attacks
nodes.content: Phishing is manipulation where attackers impersonate trusted entities (your agency, IT department, grant funders) to trick you into revealing passwords, clicking malicious links, or opening infected files. Attackers target social service organizations because staff are mission-driven and act quickly on urgent requests. Red flags include: (1) unexpected requests for passwords or personal data, (2) urgent language creating pressure, (3) links or attachments from unknown sources, (4) slight misspellings in email addresses, and (5) requests to bypass normal procedures. Always verify through a trusted phone number, never using contact info from the suspicious message.

nodes.title: Example: The Urgent Client Request
nodes.content: Sarah received an email appearing to be from her supervisor: 'Urgent intake for family in crisis. Need emergency contact verification ASAP—reply with login credentials to process.' The address was 'supervisor@socialsevice.com' (missing 's' in 'service'). Sarah noticed the unusual credential request and called her supervisor directly, discovering it was a phishing email targeting the agency. The attacker hoped to access case files. Sarah learned: legitimate supervisors never ask for passwords via email; verify contacts by calling a known number; and urgent pressure is a classic manipulation tactic.

nodes.title: Integrating Security Into Daily Practice
nodes.content: You've explored how to protect client data, secure your accounts, and recognize threats. Cybersecurity isn't a separate task—it's part of your professional identity. Every day: lock your screen when you step away, use strong unique passwords with multi-factor authentication, verify unexpected requests before responding, delete old files according to policy, and report suspicious activity immediately. Your agency has security policies and an IT or security team—use them. Protecting client data protects clients; maintaining account security prevents impersonation; recognizing threats stops attacks before they spread. These practices are the foundation of responsible, ethical social work in the digital age.

nodes.title: Course Complete: You're a Cybersecurity-Aware Social Worker
nodes.content: Congratulations! You've learned practical, ethical cybersecurity practices that directly protect your clients and your profession. You understand how to secure sensitive data, guard your access, and recognize manipulation attempts. More importantly, you recognize that security is inseparable from social work ethics. Your clients trust you with their most vulnerable information—maintaining that trust through strong security practices is a core part of your professional responsibility. Return to these practices daily, stay vigilant, and never hesitate to ask your IT or security team when you're unsure.
